# Sodium Management

Practical Approach to Hyponatremia (uptodate)

CSW SIADH DI Increased Decreased Increased Urine output Low Low High Serum Na

High High Low Urine Na Normal/Low Low High Serum Osm

Normal/Low High Low Urine Osm Low High Normal/Low CVP Normal High Low ADH

Estimate of 1L of any saline infusion on serum sodium Change in serum sodium = [infusion sodium concentration (mmol/L) – serum sodium concentration (mmol/L)] / (total body water +
1) Sodium concentration (mmol/L) Infusion 855 5% NaCl in water 513 3% NaCl in water 154 0.9% NaCl in water 130 Lactate Ringer 77 0.45% NaCl in water 34 0.2% NaCl in D5W 0 D5W

Sodium Repletion
- Calculate sodium deficit = 0.6 x body weight x (desired [Na] – current [Na])

- Use 0.5 for women/elderly men or 0.45 for elderly women
- Na check q4h (ICU) or q4-6h (floor
- Target repletion rate: 6 mEq/L/day

Low serum sodium (<135 mEq/L)

Hyperglycemia present?

Calculate corrected sodium*

Yes

Could extra solutes be present in serum:
- Is pt postop after prostate or uterine surgery?
- Has the pt received IV mannitol or IVIG

Hyperglycemia induced hyponatremia Low

Normal No

Reason to suspect pseudohyponatremia (due to hyperlipidemia or hyperproteinemia):
- Is serum lipemic?
- Is pt jaundiced?
- Is there history of plasma cell dyscrasia?

No

Measure serum osmolality

Yes

No

Yes

Hypotonic hyponatremia

Normal or high

Isoosmolar or hyperosmolar hyponatremia:
- TURP and hysteroscopy can be associated with absorption of irrigation solutions (glycine, sorbitol)
- If suspected pseudohyponatremia, have sodium measured by direct potentiometry. If normal, measure total protein and lipids. Low (<280 mosmol/kg)

GFR substantially impaired?

Yes Renal failure No Use of thiazide diuretic?

Yes

Stop thiazide

Hyponatremia resolved? Yes Thiazide induced hyponatremia

No

No Edema? (peripheral or pulmonary edema, or ascites)

Yes Low effective arterial blood volume (heart failure, cirrhosis)

Signs of hypovolemia (low BP, orthostatic)?

Yes No Yes Measure urine sodium Measure urine sodium and urine osmolality

Low (<25 mEq/L)

High (>40 mEq/L)

(25-40 mEq/L)

Hypovolemic hyponatremia Extrarenal losses (GI, third space, diuretics)

Infuse isotonic saline (ex. 1L over 1hr) Remeasure serum sodium

Hypovolemic hyponatremia Renal losses

Review hx and meds AM cortisol and ACTH stim test

Low cortisol Positive ACTH stim test

Using diuretics

Head injury Cranial surgery

Primary adrenal insufficiency (glucocorticoid and mineralocorticoid deficiency)

Diuretic induced

Cerebral salt wasting

Urine osmolality <100?

No

Urine sodium >40?

Yes Urine osmolality measured AFTER therapy initiated?

Yes Recovery from one of following:
- Mild hypovolemia (given isotonic fluids)
- Hypopituitarism (given glucocorticoids)

No

Rapid water consumption?

Yes

No High fluid, low protein diet:
- “Beer potomania”
- “Tea and toast diet”

Self  induced water intoxication:
- Psychosis
- Endurance event
- Ecstasy use

Ensure sodium intake >150 mEq over next 24 hrs (ex. 1L isotonic saline over 1 or more hrs)

Remeasure urine osmolality and sodium

No

Urine Na <40 or urine osm <100 Urine Na >40 or urine osm >100 Hypovolemic hyponatremia

Evaluate for glucocorticoid deficiency and hypothyroidism

Yes

AM cortisol and ACTH stim test abnormal?

Yes Glucocorticoid deficiency No

TSH very elevated?

Yes Severe hypothyroidism

No

SIADH or nephrogenic SIADH evaluate underlying etiology

Practical Approach to Hyponatremia (uptodate)

*Add 2 mEq/L to the serum sodium for every 100 mg/dL of serum glucose above the normal value
